steps.title: Find the key signal
steps.content: The conclusion is that “contrasting markings were a deterrent”—meaning spiders should avoid attacking striped termites and instead target solid-color ones. The data must show spiders preferring solid colors over stripes, regardless of which termite caught their initial attention.

steps.title: Match the evidence to the choices
steps.content: Reading across the table rows (each showing what spiders attacked after initially looking at each cape type): spiders that first looked at solid black attacked solid colors 86% of the time and stripes only 13%; those that first looked at solid white attacked solid colors 100% and stripes 0%; those that first looked at striped attacked solid colors 75% and stripes 25%. All three rows show a large majority attacking solid-color capes, not striped ones. Choice A describes specific percentages without demonstrating the overall pattern, choice B falsely claims striped capes attracted a majority (only 25%), and choice D captures just one row. Choice C directly states that across all initial-interest types, the large majority of attacks targeted solid-color capes—the exact pattern needed to support a deterrent effect for striped markings. That matches choice C — C is correct.
